metadata.summary: Continuous monitoring of competitor signals into digests, threat assessments, and battlecards. Live delta pipeline driven by specialized subagents replaces stale snapshots.

content.title: Competitive Intelligence
content.summary: Point-in-time competitor snapshots go stale within weeks. Continuous CI pipelines that separate mechanical collection (Haiku) from strategic synthesis (Opus) achieve 85-95% time reduction and measurably improve sales win rates. Battlecards older than 14 days with confident tone are worse than no battlecard.

why.p: Point-in-time competitor snapshots go stale within weeks. Continuous CI pipelines that separate mechanical collection (Haiku) from strategic synthesis (Opus) achieve 85-95% time reduction and measurably improve sales win rates. Battlecards older than 14 days with confident tone are worse than no battlecard.

list.item: Live B2B/SaaS market where competitors ship weekly and pricing changes often.
Sales team needs current battlecards (deal cycle > 30 days exposes stale data fast).
Product roadmap decisions blocked on feature parity or differentiation gaps.
Funding round, M&A, or executive hire signals need to surface within 24h.
You already have 3+ named direct competitors plus stable URLs to track.
Pre-PMF or category-creation phase—competitors are not the bottleneck, customer interviews are.
Fewer than 5 known competitors—manual quarterly snapshot beats infrastructure overhead.
Highly regulated/closed markets (defense, sealed bids) where public signals are noise.
Personal projects with no GTM motion—output has no consumer.
When the team will not act on alerts (CI without a sales/product action loop is theater).

section.name: signal-sources-and-frequencies
section.p: Signal sources, monitoring frequencies, platform landscape, and CI evolution from reactive to predictive.

rule.statement: Monitor each signal category at its natural change rate: pricing and funding weekly, feature launches and website changes daily, hiring and content/SEO monthly.
rule.rationale: Polling too slowly misses time-sensitive funding and pricing signals. Polling too frequently on slow-moving signals wastes quota and generates noise.

list.item: Pricing changes: Crayon, Klue + manual — weekly
Feature launches: Product Hunt, Twitter — daily
Hiring patterns: LinkedIn — monthly
Funding/M&A news: Crunchbase, AlphaSense — weekly
Customer reviews: G2, Capterra — weekly
Content/SEO changes: Ahrefs, SEMrush — monthly
Website changes: Visualping, Crayon — daily

section.name: ci-platform-comparison
section.p: Choosing the right platform tier matched to scale and workflow.

rule.statement: Pick a platform tier matched to your scale: Crayon/Klue for mid-market automation and battlecard distribution; Contify Athena for agentic unstructured-content analysis; AlphaSense for filings and expert-call transcripts.
rule.rationale: Over-engineering CI infrastructure before you have 3+ named competitors and an action loop wastes budget. Under-investing means alerts are never acted on.

list.item: Crayon: CI automation, battlecard generation, partial REST API
Klue: Market intelligence distribution to Salesforce/Slack, partial REST API
Contify Athena (2025): Agentic AI engine for unstructured-content reasoning
AlphaSense: Multi-source intel (filings, transcripts, expert calls), API + summaries
Kompyte (Semrush): Real-time site/SEO delta tracking at scale, webhook + API
Valona Intelligence: Multilingual CI for EU/APAC competitors, limited API

section.name: ci-evolution-2025-2026
section.p: Current (2025): Reactive and descriptive—tells you what competitors did after they did it. Emerging (2026): Predictive and prescriptive—anticipates competitor moves and recommends strategic responses via multi-agent pipelines.

rule.statement: Build toward predictive CI by feeding historical event logs into a threat-scoring model calibrated quarterly against actual lost deals.
rule.rationale: Descriptive CI is table stakes. Predictive CI compresses the gap between a competitor signal and your strategic response from weeks to hours.

section.name: output-cadence
section.p: Monitoring outputs and distribution schedules tailored to different stakeholder needs.

list.item: Weekly digest: product team—Delta events per competitor, scored by signal type
Monthly threat assessment: leadership—Evidence-based threat score 1-5, recommended action
Quarterly feature comparison: sales, marketing—Full feature matrix vs competitors
Quarterly strategic report: executive—Scenario planning, market shifts
Real-time battlecards: sales reps—Auto-regenerated on high-severity events, TTL 14 days

rule.statement: Stamp every battlecard with a generation date and enforce a 14-day TTL; a 2-week-old battlecard with confident tone is worse than no battlecard.
rule.rationale: Sales reps trust dated artifacts. Stale-but-confident claims actively damage deals.

section.name: ci-monitoring-framework
section.p: Detailed monitoring framework covering signal sources and natural change rates across competitive signals.

section.name: subagent-roles
section.p: Six-subagent pipeline separating mechanical collection from strategic synthesis, with fact-checker and prompt patterns.

rule.statement: Assign Haiku to mechanical collection and classification; Sonnet to synthesis and battlecard writing; Opus to threat assessment and scenario planning—never invert this.
rule.rationale: Mixing reasoning-grade models into collection tasks adds 10x cost with no marginal accuracy gain. Mixing fast models into threat analysis produces hallucinated strategic claims.

list.item: ci-collector (Haiku): Polls YAML watchlist (URLs, keywords, RSS, review sites). Emits normalized JSON events. No reasoning, no synthesis.
ci-classifier (Haiku/Sonnet): Tags each event as pricing | feature | hire | funding | review | content. Discards noise.
ci-synthesizer (Sonnet): Clusters week-of events per competitor, drafts digest with cited sources.
ci-threat-analyst (Opus): Generates threat assessment, "so what" recommendations, scenario planning. Run weekly or on funding/M&A signals.
ci-battlecard-writer (Sonnet): Regenerates per-competitor battlecards from latest digest + win/loss notes. Output to Salesforce, Highspot, or Slack.
ci-fact-checker (Sonnet): Adversarial pass—every claim must cite a fetched URL with a date. Flags unsupported claims before distribution.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: Structured prompt templates for collector and threat analyst agents, enforcing no-inference and evidence-floor constraints.

example.title: Collector prompt with structured output
example.note: XML structured output, no inference allowed:
example.code: <task>collect</task> <watchlist>{{ yaml_watchlist }}</watchlist> <since>{{ last_run_iso }}</since> <output_schema>{"events":[{"competitor","signal_type","url","fetched_at","raw_excerpt","delta_summary"}]}</output_schema> <rules>NO inference. NO summarization beyond delta. If page unreachable, emit error event.</rules>

example.title: Threat analyst prompt with evidence floor
example.note: Opus with evidence floor and citation requirement:
example.code: <role>CI threat analyst</role> <digest>{{ weekly_digest }}</digest> <our_positioning>{{ positioning_md }}</our_positioning> <deliverable>threat_score (1-5), evidence (>=3 cited events), recommended_action, decision_owner</deliverable> <constraint>Cite event_id for every factual claim. If evidence < 3, return INSUFFICIENT_DATA.</constraint>

section.name: fact-checker-gate
section.p: Mandatory fact-checking step to prevent citation hallucination and ensure source validity before distribution.

rule.statement: Every digest and battlecard must pass a fact-checker agent that HEAD-requests every cited URL before the document is distributed.
rule.rationale: LLMs invent URLs like /pricing/enterprise that never existed. Publishing an invented citation to a sales rep destroys trust in the entire CI function.

antipattern.description: Skipping the fact-checker to save tokens on the synthesis pass.
antipattern.reason: A single hallucinated feature claim in a battlecard that contradicts the competitor's actual product will be caught by a prospect, not the agent.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes and mitigation strategies when building CI agents.

list.item: Empty-body trap: JS-rendered SPAs return shells; Firecrawl/Jina or Playwright required. Alert on body-length variance > 80%.
Cookie/paywall: SaaS pricing pages 302-redirect to login. Treat 30x/40x as a signal, not silence.
Translation noise: site i18n switches based on geo IP; pin the locale via header to prevent fake "site-redesign" alarms.
Citation hallucination: model invents URLs. Fact-checker must HEAD every URL before publishing.
Recency bias: weekly digest over-weights last 2 days. Normalize event volume per day before clustering.
Compounded summarization: digest-of-digest-of-digest loses the "so what". Always re-ground synthesizer prompts on raw events, not previous digests.
Stop-words in change detection: HTML diff catches CSS/build-hash churn. Strip script/style tags and timestamps before fingerprinting.
Rate limits + retries: agent loops on 429 burning tokens. Wrap fetches with exponential backoff and a circuit breaker.
LinkedIn ToS: scraping can get the company account banned. Route through compliant providers; never use a personal cookie.
Internal politics: a "high threat" assessment naming a partner can blow up deals. Add a redaction step + named-entity allowlist before distribution.
Confirmation bias prompts: "Is competitor X a threat?" yields yes. Use neutral framing: "Score evidence-based threat 1-5; default 1".

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Agent-friendly tools for content extraction, page diffing, review scraping, and data sources.

list.item: firecrawl / jina reader: LLM-ready page extraction with JS rendering—primary fallback for SPA pages
playwright: Custom JS-rendered scrape when API and jina both fail
rss-parser (npm) / feedparser (py): Newsroom + blog ingestion—use RSS before scraping
visualping: Diff alerts on rendered pages—cheap raw signal, no synthesis
phantombuster: LinkedIn hiring/post extraction—use with proxy rotation and respect ToS
apify actors: Headless scrape for G2, LinkedIn, Product Hunt—pay-per-run
crunchbase API: Funding/M&A/leadership—ground truth for firmographic data
ahrefs / semrush API: SEO/SEM moves, top pages, keyword shifts
g2 Buyer Intent API: Review velocity and intent signals

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Operational practices for building and sustaining a production CI pipeline.

rule.statement: Emit one event per delta, never aggregate inside the collector—keeps the audit trail clean and lets the synthesizer see the raw timeline.
rule.rationale: Aggregated events hide the sequence of competitor moves. The synthesizer needs the timeline to detect acceleration patterns.

rule.statement: Force every LLM claim to carry an event_id reference; reject digests that fail the fact-checker pass.
rule.rationale: Unsourced claims are liabilities in sales conversations. Require provenance.

rule.statement: Calibrate threat scores against actual lost deals quarterly; recalibrate the prompt if the score does not predict losses.
rule.rationale: An uncalibrated threat score becomes a noise source. Quarterly calibration ties CI to revenue impact.

rule.statement: Battlecard TTL ≤ 14 days. Stamp generation date prominently; sales reps trust dated artifacts.
rule.rationale: Stale battlecards with confident tone damage credibility. A 14-day TTL enforces freshness.

rule.statement: Separate "what changed" (Haiku, mechanical) from "so what" (Opus, judgment). Mixing them costs 10x and adds hallucination.
rule.rationale: Opus reasoning on raw HTML diffs is wasteful and noisy. Haiku distills the signal; Opus interprets the pattern.

rule.statement: Subscribe to competitor changelog/RSS first; scraping is the fallback. APIs > RSS > scrape > screenshot diff.
rule.rationale: APIs are reliable and ToS-compliant. Scraping is fragile and legally ambiguous. Use the most reliable source available per competitor.

rule.statement: Track yourself with the same pipeline. Catches what your own marketing site exposes to competitors.
rule.rationale: CI is bidirectional. What you publish about pricing, hiring, and product reveals your strategy to the same tools your competitors use.

rule.statement: Keep a "do-not-track" list (legal, ex-employees, personal blogs) to avoid ToS and ethical issues.
rule.rationale: Scraping without guardrails creates legal and reputational risk.

rule.statement: Run win/loss interviews monthly and feed transcripts back into battlecard prompts—review-site sentiment alone is a lagging indicator.
rule.rationale: Win/loss data grounds CI in real deal outcomes, not sentiment signals.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Known failure modes and anti-patterns in competitive intelligence automation.

antipattern.description: Hallucinated synthesis: LLMs invent competitor features when scraped page is paywalled or JS-rendered and returns empty body.
antipattern.reason: Always require source citation per claim. Reject unsourced assertions.

antipattern.description: Pricing pages hidden behind "Contact Sales"—daily monitors generate false silence.
antipattern.reason: Cross-check G2/Capterra and customer reviews instead for pricing signal.

antipattern.description: Translation drift in non-English markets—Crayon/Klue auto-translate loses nuance.
antipattern.reason: Use Valona for multilingual CI or manual review per region.

antipattern.description: Regulatory limits: scraping LinkedIn at scale violates ToS; LinkedIn API is gated.
antipattern.reason: Use Phantombuster/Apify with proxy rotation and honor robots.txt or pay for Crunchbase/AlphaSense API.

antipattern.description: Survivorship bias in win-rate claims (16% → 45%)—usually conflates CI rollout with sales enablement and pricing changes.
antipattern.reason: Audit methodology before accepting vendor case study claims. Tie CI improvements to quarterly lost-deal analysis.

template.purpose: Minimal collector loop: fetches watchlist URLs via Jina reader, emits delta events to NDJSON.
Input config for ci-collector: competitor name, URLs, signal type.
